1: "If you fully obey the LORD your God and carefully follow all his commands I give you today, the LORD your God will set you high above all the nations on earth" — the opening condition frames the entire blessing-and-curse structure of chapter 28 as the consequence-catalogue of a suzerainty treaty. The great empires of the ANE formalized their treaties with vassal states using a fixed structure: preamble, historical prologue, stipulations, deposit and reading instructions, witness list, and then blessing/curse lists. Hittite suzerainty treaties (14th-13th c. BCE) contain elaborate curse-catalogues; Esarhaddon's vassal treaties (672 BCE) contain multi-page curse-lists invoking dozens of ANE deities. Deuteronomy 28 is the most sustained parallel to this treaty-curse genre in the ancient world, but with YHWH as the sole divine witness and enforcer. The opening conditional makes clear that the blessings are not automatic gifts but covenant-consequences.
2: "All these blessings will come upon you and overtake you if you obey the LORD your God" — the verb "overtake" (hasigucha) is the same word used for the curses in v15: "all these curses will come upon you and overtake you." The symmetry is precise — blessings pursue the obedient as curses pursue the disobedient. The blessings are not passive receipts but active forces that pursue and catch the covenant-keeper. The relentless-grace image is the counterpart to the relentless-judgment of the curse section.
3: "You will be blessed in the city and blessed in the country" — the first of the seven blessing-couplets that mirror exactly the seven curse-couplets of vv16-19. The city/country pairing is a merism encompassing all human habitations — sedentary urban life and pastoral/agricultural rural life. The complete geographical coverage signals that the blessing is total and pervasive: no sphere of life is excluded from YHWH's abundance when the covenant is kept.
4: "The fruit of your womb will be blessed, and the crops of your land, and the young of your livestock" — the three primary realms of ancient agricultural life: human fertility, crop fertility, and livestock fertility. All three represent the continuation and abundance of life. In the ANE, these three domains were precisely what the fertility deities (Baal, Asherah, Dagan) were credited with providing. Deuteronomy's blessings make a direct counter-claim: YHWH, not the Canaanite fertility pantheon, controls the womb, the ground, and the herd.
5: "Your basket and your kneading bowl will be blessed" — the basket (tene, H2935) and kneading bowl (mishereth, H4863) are the two instruments of food processing: the basket that collects and carries grain and fruit, and the bowl where dough is mixed for bread. The blessing of these implements stands for the blessing of all food preparation — from harvest to table. The inclusion of domestic implements alongside crops signals that the blessing reaches into the domestic sphere of daily labor, not just the field.
6: "You will be blessed when you come in and blessed when you go out" — the come-in/go-out merism covers all of life's movement: entry and departure, home and road, rest and work. This is the complete temporal and spatial coverage of blessing. The same phrase is used in Psalms 121:8 ("The LORD will watch over your coming and going both now and forevermore") as a comprehensive statement of divine protection. The blessing is not situational but accompanies the covenant-keeper through all transitions.
7: "The LORD will cause the enemies who rise up against you to be defeated before you. They will come at you from one direction but flee in seven" — the seven-direction flight: the attacking enemy, however organized, scatters in seven directions (a numerical symbol of completeness and disarray). The military reversal is YHWH-caused (yiten YHWH) — Israel does not achieve its military victories through superior tactics or armament but through covenant-relationship. The one-against-many attack becoming seven-way retreat is the structural inversion of the siege-and-defeat threat of the curses (vv25,49-52).
8: "The LORD will send a blessing on your barns and on everything you put your hand to" — the storehouse (meguroteicha, H618 — grain storage pits or storehouses) receives the divine blessing. Storage facilities represent the security of future provision: full barns mean safety from famine. The phrase "everything you put your hand to" (bemishlach yadecha) appears throughout Deuteronomy (Deuteronomy 12:7,18; Deuteronomy 15:10; Deuteronomy 23:20; Deuteronomy 28:8,20) as a formula for work's productivity. The blessing is economically comprehensive — from the current harvest to the stored reserve to future labor.
9: "The LORD will establish you as his holy people, as he swore to you, if you keep his commands and walk in his ways" — the establishment as holy people (yequimcha YHWH lo le-am qadosh) is the covenant-identity goal: Israel's status as YHWH's holy nation (Exodus 19:6) is actualized through covenant obedience, not merely declared. The oath-appeal grounds the promise in YHWH's prior commitment to the patriarchs — the blessings are not arbitrary incentives but the fulfillment of ancient promises. The conditional "if" (im) maintains the covenant's bilateral structure.
10: "All the peoples of the earth will see that you bear the name of the LORD, and they will be in awe of you" — the nations' reaction to Israel's covenant prosperity: the phrase "bear the name of the LORD" (shem YHWH niqra aleicha) describes ownership and protection. To bear someone's name in the ANE was to belong to them and be under their authority and protection. Israel bearing YHWH's name means the nations recognize Israel as YHWH's possession — and respond with awe (uyarau mimeka). The international witness-function of covenant blessing is one of Deuteronomy's consistent theological points (cf. Deuteronomy 4:6-8: the nations observe Israel's wisdom and nearness to God).
11: "The LORD will grant you abundant prosperity" — the covenant prosperity (hotirekha YHWH letovah) is not merely individual wealth but communal flourishing. The three domains of v4 (womb, land, livestock) are restated here as the content of abundance. The verb hotir (make abundant, make surplus) points to more than subsistence — the covenant-community living in full prosperity has resources that overflow its own needs, enabling the generosity that the social laws require.
12: "The LORD will open his abundant storehouse — the sky — to send rain on your land in season and to bless all your work. You will lend to many nations but not borrow from any" — the sky as YHWH's storehouse (otzer tov): YHWH controls the rain-store that the Baal cult claimed to control. The seasonal rain (geshem artzecha be-itto) is YHWH's gift, not Baal's. The economic consequence of agricultural abundance: Israel becomes a lending nation, not a borrowing one — the reversal of the creditor-debtor relationship that marks political dominance (Proverbs 22:7). The Assyrian and Babylonian demand for tribute from vassal Judah was the historical fulfillment of the curse-version of this promise (v44).
13: "The LORD will make you the head, not the tail; you will always rise, never fall" — the head/tail political metaphor: the head leads and directs; the tail follows and is subordinate. In the ANE political world, great powers were heads (they imposed tribute, set terms, commanded armies) and vassal states were tails (they paid tribute, received terms, supplied troops). The blessing transforms Israel's political position from peripheral to central, from dependent to independent. The "always rise, never fall" formula is a comprehensive promise of political stability and dignity — the exact inversion of what the curse describes (v43-44).
14: "And if you do not turn aside from any of the commands I give you today, to the right or to the left, by going after other gods and serving them" — the concluding condition for the blessing section: the right/left non-deviation formula (cf. Deuteronomy 5:32; Deuteronomy 17:11,20) defines faithful covenant-keeping as non-deviation from YHWH's commands. The specific threat named is idolatry: "going after other gods" (lelekhet acharei elohim acherim) is the breach that triggers the curses. The blessing section's conclusion pointing to its own negation sets up the transition to the curse section.
15: "But if you do not obey the LORD your God and do not carefully follow all his commands and statutes I give you today, all these curses will come upon you and overtake you" — the curse section (vv15-68) begins with the exact structural mirror of the blessing's opening (v1). The curses overtake (hasigucha) the disobedient as the blessings pursue the obedient (v2). The 54 curse-verses versus 14 blessing-verses (almost 4:1 ratio) reflects covenant-legal realism: the specific consequences of disobedience require more elaboration than the general consequence of obedience, because the various forms of failure need specific documentation. ANE treaty curse-lists similarly dwarf their blessing counterparts.
16: "You will be cursed in the city and cursed in the country" — the inverted mirror of v3: the city/country merism now receives cursing instead of blessing. Verses 16-19 are exact negations of vv3-6: every sphere blessed in obedience becomes cursed in disobedience. The literary symmetry enforces the theological point: what YHWH can bless, YHWH can curse. The domains of life are not neutral or self-determining — they are responsive to the covenant relationship.
17: "Your basket and your kneading bowl will be cursed" — the inversion of v5: the food-processing implements that blessed the harvest in obedience now curse the food chain in disobedience. What is gathered is insufficient; what is prepared is inadequate. The curse reaches into domestic food-processing, not just agricultural production.
18: "The fruit of your womb will be cursed, along with the crops of your land, the calves of your herds, and the lambs of your flocks" — the inversion of v4: the three fertility domains (human, agricultural, livestock) that were blessed in obedience now fall under curse. In ANE fertility theology, the curse of womb and ground was the ultimate terror. The Baals and Asherahs were worshiped precisely to prevent these curses; Deuteronomy's irony is that worshiping them instead of YHWH (v14's breach) triggers the curse of the very domains they claimed to protect.
19: "You will be cursed when you come in and cursed when you go out" — the inversion of v6: the complete coverage of all movement and activity that was blessed in obedience now receives curse in disobedience. No moment of transition, no spatial movement, carries anything but the covenant's judgment. The comprehensive cursing of movement mirrors the comprehensive blessing of v6 but inverts its outcome completely.
20: "The LORD will send on you disaster, confusion, and failure in everything you undertake, until you are quickly destroyed because of the evil you have done in forsaking him" — three curse-agents: me'era (disaster/curse, the divine sanction), mehumah (confusion, panic), and miger'et (failure, rebuke). The three operate together to undermine every human project: disaster hits externally, confusion disrupts internally, and failure eliminates productivity. The ground for judgment: "the evil you have done in forsaking him" (maasecha asher azavtani).
21: "The LORD will plague you with disease until he has wiped you out from the land you are entering to possess" — the land-possession language of the conquest is reversed: Israel enters the land to possess it, but the land becomes the site of destruction. The plague (dever, H1698 — epidemic disease, often the fifth of the Exodus plagues) will not stop until the land is cleared of the disobedient Israel. This is the historical pattern fulfilled in 722 BCE (Assyrian deportation of the northern kingdom) and 586 BCE (Babylonian exile).
22: "The LORD will strike you with tuberculosis, fever, inflammation, scorching heat, and drought — with blight and mildew" — seven specific disease and agricultural curses: shachefet (wasting disease/tuberculosis), qaddachat (fever), dalleqet (inflammation), chamum (scorching heat), cherev (sword or drought), shiddafon (blight), yeraqon (mildew/yellowing of grain). The seven-fold listing mirrors the seven blessings; the agricultural diseases (blight, mildew) attack the crops directly while the bodily diseases attack the people. When the covenant breaks, both people and land suffer simultaneously.
23: "The sky above you will be like bronze, the ground beneath you like iron" — the climate-reversal curse: the sky that was YHWH's abundant storehouse of rain (v12) becomes impenetrable bronze — no moisture descends. The ground that was YHWH's gift to Israel becomes unyielding iron — no root penetrates, no seed sprouts. Rain was already scarce in Canaan compared to Egypt's Nile irrigation; a drought curse in this landscape was catastrophic. The ANE rain-god was supposed to prevent exactly this; YHWH sends it as covenant judgment.
24: "The LORD will turn the rain of your land into dust and powder; it will come down from the sky until you are destroyed" — the inversion of productive rain: instead of life-giving water from heaven, dust and fine powder rain down. The image may refer to the sirocco (khamsin), the hot, desiccating east wind that blows across Canaan from the desert — it brings dust and withers vegetation. YHWH sends this in place of the spring rains, reversing the weather patterns on which Canaanite agriculture depended.
25: "The LORD will cause you to be routed before your enemies. You will march out against them from one direction but flee in seven" — the precise inversion of v7: the blessing gave Israel military victory by scattering enemies in seven directions; the curse gives enemies military victory by scattering Israel in seven directions. The same "one direction in, seven directions out" formula is applied with the roles reversed. The same YHWH who grants military victory to the covenant-keeper directs military defeat against the covenant-breaker.
26: "Your dead bodies will become food for all the birds and the wild animals, and there will be no one to frighten them away" — the unburied dead was among the most shameful possible fates in the ANE. Proper burial was essential for honor and for the peace of the dead's spirit in many ancient cultures. The curse of unburied bodies eaten by scavengers inverted the ANE honor-death ritual entirely. Jeremiah repeatedly describes this curse as fulfilled in the Babylonian destruction (Jeremiah 7:33; Deuteronomy 8:1-2; Deuteronomy 16:4; Deuteronomy 34:20). The detail "no one to frighten them away" indicates complete social collapse: no surviving community member can perform even this minimal protective duty.
27: "The LORD will afflict you with the boils of Egypt and with tumors, festering sores, and an itch that cannot be healed" — the Egypt-plague diseases in reverse: Israel sent to Canaan to escape Egypt's diseases now finds those same diseases following them in Canaan. The "boils of Egypt" (shechin mitzrayim) — the sixth Exodus plague (Exodus 9:10-11) — become a covenant curse in Israel's own land. The incurability prevents even medical management of the suffering. The return of Egypt's plagues against Israel in their own land is one of the curse section's recurring ironies.
28: "The LORD will afflict you with madness, blindness, and confusion of mind" — three psychological/neurological curses: shigga'on (madness), ivvaron (blindness), and timhon levav (confusion of mind/bewilderment of heart). The external agricultural and military curses now reach into the inner person — the faculties of perception, reason, and orientation are disabled. The practical consequences follow in v29: they grope in darkness at midday and cannot accomplish any goal.
29: "At midday you will grope about like a blind person in the dark" — the blindness-curse renders daylight useless: the covenant-breaker cannot see even in full sun, groping like a blind person in darkness. The reversal: the sun that was YHWH's creation-gift provides no guidance to those under the curse. The parallel to Egypt's plague of darkness (Exodus 10:21-23) is deliberate: the Egyptians groped in darkness while Israelites had light; now Israelites grope while others see clearly.
30: "You will be pledged to a woman, but another man will sleep with her. You will build a house, but you will not live in it. You will plant a vineyard, but you will not even begin to enjoy its fruit" — the three frustration-curses parallel the exemptions from military service in Deuteronomy 20:5-7 (the man who built a house but hasn't dedicated it; planted a vineyard but not enjoyed it; betrothed a woman but not married her). The labor investment is made but the enjoyment is seized by another. In the curse-context, the war itself delivers these deprivations universally.
31: "Your ox will be slaughtered in your sight, but you will eat none of it. Your donkey will be stolen right in front of you and will not be returned. Your sheep will be given to your enemies, and no one will rescue them" — the helpless-observation curse: the covenant-breaker watches his animals slaughtered, stolen, and given away and cannot prevent any of it. The ox (draft animal for plowing), donkey (transport), and sheep (wool and meat) are the three foundational livestock of the Israelite economy. Their loss means agricultural collapse, transportation failure, and food/clothing deprivation.
32: "Your sons and daughters will be given to another nation while you watch — your eyes straining with longing for them all day long, but powerless to do anything" — the deportation of children as the deepest personal curse: the loss of children through deportation is worse than their death because the parents survive in helpless longing. The historical fulfillment: the Babylonian deportation of Judah scattered families across the empire, separating children from parents permanently in many cases (Lamentations 1:5; Deuteronomy 5:2-3).
33: "A nation you do not know will devour the produce of your soil and all your labor. You will be oppressed and crushed relentlessly" — the foreign-nation harvest curse: the land's produce, cultivated by Israel's labor, is consumed by a foreign nation that Israel doesn't even know. The emphasis on "you do not know" (asher lo yadatta) heightens the alienation. The oppression and crushing are relentless — describing precisely the Assyrian and Babylonian tributary system imposed on Israel and Judah.
34: "Until you are driven mad by what you see" — the accumulated vision of the curses produces madness: the covenant-breaker must watch the serial theft, deportation, and subjugation described in vv30-33, and the helpless watching eventually destroys mental stability. The verse functions as a summary-bridge between the initial curse-series (vv15-33) and the extended curse-series beginning in v36.
35: "The LORD will afflict your knees and legs with painful, incurable boils — from the sole of your foot to the top of your head" — the head-to-toe body-curse: the boils that covered the Egyptians in Exodus 9:10 now cover Israel from feet to head. The specific focus on knees and legs indicates the inability to stand, walk, or flee — the physical embodiment of the paralysis described in vv29-34. The incurability matches v27's formulation: medical intervention cannot break the covenant curse because it is a divine judgment.
36: "The LORD will drive you and the king you set over you to a nation unknown to you or your ancestors, where you will serve gods of wood and stone" — the exile of both people and king: the king, promised in Deuteronomy 17:14-20 as a conditional institution, will share the people's exile fate. The exile-destination is unknown — not Egypt (whose subjugation Israel already knew) but a new, alien empire. The worst element of exile: living in contexts of forced idol-worship. The irony: they abandoned YHWH to serve idols at home; in exile, they are surrounded by idol-worship they did not choose.
37: "You will become a thing of horror and an object of ridicule and mockery among all the nations where the LORD drives you" — the honor-shame dimensions of exile: in the ANE's honor-culture, national defeat was a statement about the defeated nation's god. The "object of ridicule and mockery" (mashal ulsheninah) was the proverbial comparison by which other nations discussed disaster: "become like Israel" as a byword for catastrophic defeat. The Psalms of Lament preserve this experience (Psalms 44:13-16; Deuteronomy 79:4; Lamentations 2:15-16).
38: "You will plant much seed but harvest little, because locusts will eat it" — the first of the agricultural-frustration curses (vv38-42): work without reward. The locust-plague was the most devastating agricultural disaster in the ancient Near East — the eighth Exodus plague (arbeh) could strip a field completely in hours. Joel 1-2 depicts an actual locust plague as both historical disaster and eschatological judgment. The covenant-breaker plants and sows in hope but harvests nothing.
39: "You will plant and tend vineyards but drink no wine and gather no grapes, because worms will eat them" — the vine-curse: worms (tola'at, H8438) destroy the vineyards before harvest. The vine was Israel's most prominent agricultural symbol (Deuteronomy 8:8; Isaiah 5:1-7; John 15:1) — the blessing and fruitfulness of the land made visible. Its destruction by worms is a slow internal destruction that renders the vine useless while still appearing alive. The vine-without-fruit becomes a prominent prophetic image (Isaiah 5:2,4; Jeremiah 2:21; Ezekiel 15:1-8).
40: "You will have olive trees throughout your land but will not use the oil, because the olives will drop off" — olive oil was fundamental to the ancient Israelite economy: food, fuel for lamps, skin ointment, and ritual anointing. An olive tree required years of cultivation before bearing fruit. The curse is that the trees remain but the olives fall prematurely, making the oil unavailable. The effort investment in maintaining olive orchards is entirely lost.
41: "You will have sons and daughters but you will not keep them, because they will go into captivity" — the child-deportation curse restated from v32: even the children born during the covenant-breaking period will not remain in Israel's household but will be taken captive. The covenant-breaker cannot build a new generation to replace what has been lost. The future is cut off along with the present.
42: "Swarms of locusts will take over all your trees and the crops of your land" — the locust-conclusion to the agricultural curse series: after the specific losses (grain, vine, olive, children), the comprehensive locust invasion devours everything else. The phrase "take over" (yirash) uses the dispossession language of conquest — the locust-swarm inherits the land that Israel inherited from the Canaanites. The word yirash (possess, dispossess, H3423) is the same word used throughout Deuteronomy for Israel's conquest of Canaan.
43: "The foreigner living among you will rise higher and higher while you sink lower and lower" — the social-inversion curse: the ger (resident alien) who was protected and cared for by the Israelite social laws (Deuteronomy 14:29; Deuteronomy 16:11; Deuteronomy 24:17-21) now becomes Israel's superior. The social order described in the laws (Israelite hosts, foreign residents as guests requiring hospitality) is completely reversed. This is both a historical observation (in exile, Israelites became the low-status foreigners in someone else's land) and a theological judgment.
44: "He will lend to you, but you will not lend to him. He will be the head, and you will be the tail" — the precise inversion of vv12-13: the blessing promised Israel would lend to nations and be the head; the curse delivers the opposite. The resident alien, protected in Israelite law, now becomes the creditor-landlord over Israel. The head/tail hierarchy of v13 is reversed. Nehemiah's memoir documents exactly this dynamic in the Persian-period restoration (Nehemiah 5:1-5).
45: "All these curses will come upon you. They will pursue and overtake you until you are destroyed, because you did not obey the LORD your God and observe the commands and decrees he gave you" — the summary-conclusion of the first curse series: all the curses pursue and overtake — the same verb (nasag) as the blessings in v2 but with destructive outcome. The curses are as relentless as the blessings would have been. The cause is stated plainly: non-obedience (asher lo shamata) to YHWH's commands and decrees.
46: "They will be a sign and a wonder against you and your descendants forever" — the curse as a sign (ot) and wonder (mofet): the same language used for the Exodus miracles (Exodus 4:21; Deuteronomy 7:3; Deuteronomy 4:34; Deuteronomy 6:22; Deuteronomy 7:19; Deuteronomy 26:8; Deuteronomy 34:11) is applied to the curses. The plagues of Egypt were signs and wonders of redemption; the covenant-curses are signs and wonders of judgment. Both display the same divine sovereignty over human history, but in opposite directions of the covenant relationship. The "forever" indicates that the historical witness-function of the curses is permanent and international.
47: "Because you did not serve the LORD your God joyfully and gladly in the time of prosperity" — the hinge verse of chapter 28: the curses are traced not to outright apostasy but to the failure of joyful service. Israel served YHWH, but without joy (simchah vetuv levav, joyfulness and goodness of heart). The covenant was kept technically but not wholeheartedly. This verse is the Deuteronomic answer to the question "why did the exile happen even when the external forms of worship were maintained?" Malachi 1:13 captures the same failure: "What a burden!" said of worship.
48: "You will serve the enemies the LORD sends against you in hunger, thirst, nakedness, and dire poverty. He will put an iron yoke on your neck until he has destroyed you" — the service-contrast: instead of serving YHWH joyfully in prosperity (v47), Israel serves enemies in the four deprivations of conquered existence. The "iron yoke" (ol barzel) on the neck is the specific image of Assyrian conquest: Assyrian royal inscriptions describe vanquished kings as dogs on a leash or cattle under a yoke. Jeremiah employs this exact imagery for Babylon's domination of Judah (Jeremiah 28:14).
49: "The LORD will bring against you a nation from far away, from the ends of the earth, that swoops down like an eagle" — the eagle-nation: the distant empire that comes as an eagle (nesher, H5404 — eagle or vulture, the great aerial predator) swooping from the ends of the earth. The eagle imagery is used for Assyria (Isaiah 10:14) and Babylon (Ezekiel 17:3 uses the eagle for Nebuchadnezzar; Habakkuk 1:8 for the Babylonians). The phrase "ends of the earth" (miqtseh ha'aretz) emphasizes the imperial reach: no geographic distance protects the covenant-breaker. The specificity of the eagle-swooping-from-afar is remarkable in a text composed before either empire had conquered Israel.
50: "A fierce-looking nation without respect for the old or pity for the young" — the characterization of the conquering nation: harsh in appearance (az panim), without the ANE's core social-honor values. In the honor-shame culture of the ancient Near East, even war had certain protocols: respecting elders and protecting children were minimum markers of civilized conduct. The invading nation violates both — a signal of total dehumanization in the conquest. The historical Assyrian and Babylonian armies were documented in their own inscriptions as agents of total terror, including the deportation of entire populations regardless of age.
51: "They will devour the young of your livestock and the crops of your land until you are destroyed. They will leave you no grain, new wine, or olive oil, nor any calves of your herds or lambs of your flocks until they have ruined you" — the three staples of Israelite agricultural economy (grain, wine, oil — the triad of covenant blessing, Deuteronomy 7:13; Deuteronomy 11:14) are stripped to zero by the conquering army. The comprehensive economic devastation echoes the agricultural curses of vv38-40 but now by human military agency rather than natural plague.
52: "They will lay siege to all your cities throughout your land until the high, fortified walls in which you trusted fall down" — the siege-warfare curse: the fortified city was the ultimate defensive resource of an ancient state. Israel's trust in city walls rather than YHWH's protection is a recurring prophetic critique (Isaiah 22:11; Jeremiah 5:17; Habakkuk 2:9-11). The siege reduces even the strongest fortifications to rubble. Archaeological evidence of deliberate destruction of city-walls is abundant in 8th-6th c. BCE Levantine sites: Lachish (documented in 2 Kgs 18-19 and in the Lachish reliefs at Nineveh), Jerusalem (586 BCE Babylonian destruction).
53: "In the desperation of the siege your enemies impose on you, you will eat the flesh of your own children — your sons and daughters the LORD your God has given you" — the siege-cannibalism curse: the most extreme form of starvation-desperation in ancient siege warfare. This was not a theoretical curse: 2 Kings 6:28-29 records it happening during Ben-Hadad's siege of Samaria (ca. 850 BCE); Josephus (Jewish War 6.3.4) records it during Titus's siege of Jerusalem (70 CE); Lamentations 2:20 and 4:10 document it for the Babylonian siege of 586 BCE. The curse became history at least three times. The identification of the children as YHWH's gift (asher natan lecha YHWH eloheicha) intensifies the horror: the blessing of v4 (fruit of the womb) is consumed by its recipients.
54: "Even the most sensitive and refined man among you will become stingy with his brother, the wife he loves, and his surviving children" — the siege creates a reversal of the open-hand generosity commanded throughout Deuteronomy. The man who would normally embody covenant-generosity (open hand toward the poor, Deuteronomy 15:8,11) becomes the most extreme hoarder when covenant-curses bring famine through siege. The specific relationships named (brother, beloved wife, surviving children) are the innermost circle of covenant social bonds — the siege-famine destroys even these core relationships through competition over survival-food.
55: "Refusing to share with any of them the flesh of his own children that he is eating — because nothing is left" — the impossibility of normative behavior under siege conditions: the phrase "because nothing is left" (mibeli hisheir lo kol) is both factual and the theological reversal of the covenant's abundance promises. The total absence of surplus — which the covenant promised as blessing (v12) — is the material condition in which all human-social goods collapse. The covenant's social ethic requires economic margin; the curses eliminate all margin.
56: "The most delicate and sensitive woman among you — so refined she would never place her foot on the ground — will begrudge the husband she loves and her own son and daughter" — the socially elevated woman represents the highest social class in Israel (the description suggests aristocratic refinement — she has never had to walk on rough ground). If even she reaches this point, the entire society has reached it. The social stratification of vv54-57 moves from the sensitive man (v54) to the delicate woman (v56), indicating that no level of social privilege provides protection from the siege-famine's dehumanizing effects.
57: "Even the afterbirth from her womb and the children she bears — she will secretly eat them, so dire will be her need during the siege of your city" — the afterbirth (shilya, H7988) is mentioned in the Torah only here: in extremis, the most hidden, intimate biological material of childbirth becomes a food source. The secrecy (baseser) indicates shame — the act is so horrifying that even in the social collapse of siege, it must be hidden. Josephus's account of the siege of Jerusalem (Jewish War 6.3.3-4) narrates a specific incident of a woman named Mary who ate her infant during the 70 CE siege.
58: "If you do not carefully follow all the words of this law, which are written in this book, and do not revere this glorious and awesome Name — the LORD your God" — the second major condition-statement in the curse section: it introduces the final and most extreme curses (vv59-68). The phrase "this law, which is written in this book" (divrei hatorah hazot haketuvim basefer hazeh) is a self-referential moment: Deuteronomy explicitly describes itself as a written text (sefer) with legal authority — a remarkable textual self-consciousness. The "glorious and awesome Name" frames what is at stake: not merely legal compliance but honor of YHWH's character.
59: "The LORD will send you and your descendants extraordinary disasters — severe and prolonged plagues, terrible and lingering illnesses" — the escalation in the final curse series: the plagues are now described with doubled intensive adjectives (peliot veneemanut, extraordinary and confirmed; ra'ot veneemanut, terrible and confirmed). The confirmation/persistence (neemanu, from aman, to be firm, enduring) is significant — these are not passing afflictions but enduring, established divine judgments. The word-play with the covenant's Amen-structure is implicit: the covenant's confirmed promises work both ways.
60: "He will bring on you all the diseases of Egypt that you dreaded, and they will cling to you" — the Egypt-disease return (cf. v27): the diseases that YHWH promised to keep away from the obedient (Deuteronomy 7:15) are now brought back and made to cling (davaq, the same verb used for covenant-loyalty in Deuteronomy 4:4; Deuteronomy 10:20; Deuteronomy 11:22). The clinging that was covenant-faithfulness's posture toward YHWH is now the curses' posture toward the covenant-breaker. The irony: the covenant-breaker who would not cling to YHWH is clung to by Egypt's plagues.
61: "The LORD will also bring on you every kind of sickness and disaster not recorded in this Book of the Law, until you are destroyed" — the open-ended curse: not only the specific curses listed but every sickness and disaster not yet named. This is a rhetorical acknowledgment that the list cannot be complete: divine judgment exceeds any list. The self-referential "this Book of the Law" (sefer hatorah hazeh) signals Deuteronomy's awareness of itself as canonical written text.
62: "You who were as numerous as the stars in the sky will be left but few in number, because you did not obey the LORD your God" — the stars-of-the-sky comparison reverses the Abrahamic promise: Genesis 15:5 ("your offspring will be as numerous as the stars") and Deuteronomy 1:10 are directly inverted. The covenant promise of numerical multiplication, which was the fulfillment of the patriarchal blessing, becomes the measure of its loss. The historical fulfillment: the Babylonian exile of 586 BCE reduced the population of Judah from an estimated 200,000+ to a remnant of a few thousand, with the majority deported or fled to Egypt.
63: "Just as the LORD delighted in making you prosper and multiply, so he will delight in bringing you to ruin and destruction" — the most theologically startling verse in Deuteronomy: YHWH's delight (yasis YHWH) in Israel's prosperity is matched by an equal delight in Israel's ruin. The same divine pleasure that energized the covenant's blessings now energizes its curses. This is not arbitrary divine sadism but the covenant's binary structure applied to the divine character: YHWH takes his covenant seriously enough to enforce both poles with equal energy. The ANE's patron-deity model assumed the deity always favored his nation; Deuteronomy's covenant theology breaks this assumption completely.
64: "The LORD will scatter you among all nations, from one end of the earth to the other. There you will serve gods of wood and stone, which neither you nor your ancestors have known" — the diaspora-curse: dispersal to all nations is the final form of covenant judgment. The contrast with the Exodus-gathering: YHWH gathered Israel from Egypt to one land; the curse scatters Israel from one land to all nations. The wood-and-stone idol service in exile is both punishment and irony: Israel's desire for other gods (implied in v14's breach) is now imposed on them in exile without the option of YHWH's central sanctuary.
65: "Among those nations you will find no repose, no resting place for the sole of your foot. There the LORD will give you an anxious mind, eyes worn out with longing, and a despairing soul" — the exile's existential character: no noacha (rest) or margoa (resting-place). The three inner experiences of exile: lev ragaz (anxious/trembling heart), kilyon einayim (eyes worn out with longing), and deabon nefesh (soul-languishing). The longing-eyes mirror the exiled parent's eyes straining toward the horizon for children who never return (v32). The diaspora is not merely political dispossession but chronic psychological displacement.
66: "Your life will hang in the balance — you will live in constant dread, day and night, never sure of your survival" — the existential insecurity of exile: no day or night brings rest from the fear of death. The phrase "your life will hang in the balance" (vehayu chayeicha teluyim lecha mineged) may be an image of the life hanging by a thread before one's eyes. This is the experiential core of the curse: not only material deprivation but the psychological horror of perpetual insecurity. Psalm 22 and Lamentations 3 give voice to exactly this experience.
67: "In the morning you will say, 'If only it were evening!' and in the evening you will say, 'If only it were morning!' — because of the dread in your heart and the sights your eyes will see" — the temporal-inversion curse: the normal human rhythm (dreading the night, looking forward to morning) is reversed. Evening cannot bring the comfort of rest; morning cannot bring the comfort of new possibility. The exile-community cannot find relief in time's natural rhythms. This is the opposite of Psalms 30:5 ("Weeping endures for the night; joy comes in the morning") — in the curse-condition, neither morning nor evening brings the expected change.
68: "The LORD will send you back to Egypt in ships, along a route I said you would never travel again. There you will offer yourselves for sale to your enemies as male and female slaves, but no one will buy you" — the ultimate curse-climax: return to Egypt by ship. The Exodus had delivered Israel from Egyptian slavery; the curse closes the covenant's narrative arc by reversing the Exodus completely. The ships may indicate a Mediterranean sea-route to Egypt. The final indignity: offering oneself as a slave but finding no buyers — even slavery is denied. Josephus (Jewish War 6.9.2-3) notes that after the 70 CE fall of Jerusalem, Jewish captives were sold into Egyptian slavery, with many finding no buyers due to the surplus of war-captives.
